text: Welcome back to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Mohan from
Purolator. Hey Mohan. Hi, how are you? So tell us a little bit about what Purolator does.
Purolator is Canada's largest express career company and we process about 5 million packages
every day and we do both business to business and business to consumer and on the B2C side
we are tied very much to things that happen in the B2C market similar to Amazon Prime Day or Black
Friday or Cyber Monday and as we approach peak now this is our holiday season as well and we have a
lot of peaks and valleys through the year. So a lot more online shopping and e-commerce going on
that's also leading to a lot of digitization. What were some of the challenges that you had with
your existing system? You're right. In our existing legacy system we had two problems that we were
trying to solve. One was the reliability of the messages and two was the scalability of what we
had in place. So how many packages do you process every day? Yeah we process about 5 million packages
every day but like I said during our peak times we do go scale up and down so it's important that we
have that elastic scalability. Scalability is really important. I see a fantastic architecture that we're
going to dive into. Tell us how it works. Sure. Let's start with the life cycle of a package.
When a customer declares intent to ship a package they produce a label and the moment they produce a
label that information is with us in our legacy system and what we do is we take that basic information
about a package and it's a JSON message essentially. We take that and process it into an SQS in a queue
and a simple lambda which we call an ingestion lambda picks it up and essentially drops it into a
relational form into an RDS at the back that we have. So inventory of your packages is really important
can't afford to lose it so using something like SQS gives you that reliability right? Exactly we have it
totally decoupled and scalable in terms of our queues and our compute. Okay so once you know about the
package what's going on over here? Yeah so on a package there is a variety of events that happen
through the package life cycle. Typical events that happen are a pickup event where a delivery or a pickup
driver goes and picks up the package from the customer and it travels through our network through our
facilities and so on and gets to the customer's door eventually and through that life cycle it goes
through like I said a few scans in the facility as in we got the package in the hub or a terminal
and it's out for deliveries another scan that we do or an event that happens on the package and then out for
deliveries another one and delivered and signature acquired etc are all these events that happen. So I see
event processing here tell us a little bit about how that works. Yeah each event like I was telling you about
has a different connotation and a different meaning to the company and to the customer. One of the
interesting ones I'll talk about but the generic event flow goes like this again every event happens
from a handheld that the pickup or delivery driver has and that gets put into another SQS queue and another
lambda pulls it off of the queue and then again processes it into a relational database at the back but each
of these lambdas have a different flavor in what they do and what purpose they serve and that is for a pickup
event the processing is different as opposed to if you take for example a disruption scan meaning a truck broke down somewhere
in between we need to know immediately so that we can actually let the customer know in real time that there may be a
potential delay in getting the package so each of these lambdas react differently to different types of events that happen.
Yeah so different queues so you're actually separating a lot of your business logic and not coupling them given you've got so many events every day.
That's right. And of course you know you're not worrying about servers everything is completely serverless.
That's right. Great. I see S3 here what are you doing with that?

This is also an interesting use case for us and like I talked about the package travels through our network and we capture several events throughout and one of them is something we do in a terminal called redimensioning and weighing.
So an example would be if you had declared a box to be 10 by 10 and 10 kilograms and as it passes through this arc machine we re-weigh it and we redimension it.
So if we were to find that either the box was bigger or heavier or what have you then that image is captured as well as a revenue uplift scan is done on that package.
And that image gets stored into an S3 bucket and we take that image or create a link in our relational database to that image that was captured and put into an S3.
That gives you if you look at this architecture here a full view of a package and its life cycle that's very very critical to our business.
And you've obviously got a single source of truth with using RDS across that inventory as it goes.
That's right. That's fantastic. What are you thinking about next?

As you rightly mentioned if we start to process more events and different sites sets of events then we might want to think about doing some orchestration within that and we're kind of looking at potentially the use of step functions.

As well as we would like or since you said that this is our single source of truth and we rely heavily on it for consumption of the back is we would like to see if we can kind of isolate the rights and the reads and potentially scale the reads, read notes through several read copies.
So you're using read replicas over here to help scale this front end.
That's right. Exactly. And we would drive our analytics and any kind of business intelligence out of those consumers. So we're looking at doing that in the future.
That's great Mohan. It's a really good use of event driven serverless architecture to go and help your customers deliver packages.
Mohan, thank you for coming in and sharing your architecture with us today.
You're welcome. Happy to be here.
I'm happy to be here.
And thank you for joining us on This Is My Architecture.
